quiz.title: Unit 7 Quiz 12, Grade 10 Biology
quiz.subtitle: Cell Division: Advanced Concepts in Mitosis and Meiosis
quiz.subject: Biology
quiz.topic: S Phase DNA Content, Mitosis in Animal Cells, Resting Phase, Anaphase Changes, Crossing Over Stage, Chromosomal Movement, Metaphase Events, Cytokinesis Differences, Chromosome Condensation, Centromere Division, Mitosis Terminology

questions.question: In 'S' phase of the cell cycle:
options: Amount of DNA doubles in each cell.
Amount of DNA remains same in each cell.
Amount of DNA is reduced to half in each cell.
questions.explanation: During S phase (synthesis phase), DNA replication occurs, doubling the amount of DNA in each cell. However, the chromosome number remains the same; each chromosome now consists of two sister chromatids. [citation:1]

questions.question: In animal cells, the mitotic division is seen in
questions.explanation: In animals, mitotic division occurs in diploid somatic cells (body cells) for growth and repair. Haploid cells in animals are gametes (sperm and egg), which do not undergo mitosis. Some animals like male honey bees have haploid cells that divide mitotically, but this is an exception, not the rule for most animals.

questions.question: The resting phase is otherwise known as
questions.explanation: Interphase is sometimes misleadingly called the 'resting phase' because the cell is not actively dividing. However, it is actually a period of high metabolic activity, growth, and DNA replication. The cell spends about 90% of its life cycle in interphase. [citation:2]

questions.question: The movement of duplicated centrioles occur towards opposite poles of the cell in:
questions.explanation: During prophase, the duplicated centrioles (which replicated during S phase) move toward opposite poles of the cell. They organize the spindle fibers as they migrate, establishing the bipolar spindle apparatus. This movement continues into prometaphase. In G₁, centrioles haven't duplicated yet. In G₂, centrioles have duplicated but haven't begun migrating. In anaphase, centrioles are already at opposite poles pulling chromatids apart.

questions.question: Cytarabine is a chemotherapy drug that mimics cytosine, a nucleotide, and kills cancer cells by interfering with the making and repair of DNA. During which phase of the cell cycle would this drug be most likely to be integrated into DNA?
questions.explanation: Cytarabine would be most likely integrated during S phase (synthesis phase), when DNA replication occurs. Since it mimics cytosine, it gets incorporated into newly synthesized DNA strands during replication, causing chain termination and DNA damage. G₁ involves growth and protein synthesis, G₂ involves preparation for mitosis, and M phase is mitosis itself—none of these involve active DNA replication.

questions.question: Mitotic anaphase differs from metaphase because it possesses
options: same number of chromosomes and same number of chromatids
half number of chromosomes and half number of chromatids
half number of chromosomes and same number of chromatids
same number of chromosomes and half number of chromatids
questions.explanation: In metaphase, each chromosome consists of two sister chromatids. After anaphase separation, each chromosome becomes a single-chromatid chromosome. So the chromosome number remains the same (they just move to opposite poles), but each chromosome now has half the number of chromatids (one instead of two).

questions.question: Genetic recombination consequent to crossing over is a significant feature of
questions.explanation: Crossing over occurs during the pachytene stage of prophase I, when homologous chromosomes are fully paired and recombination nodules facilitate the exchange of genetic material between non-sister chromatids. [citation:5]

questions.question: The reason of chromosomal movement in the anaphase is
questions.explanation: Spindle fibers (microtubules) are responsible for chromosomal movement during anaphase. They shorten and pull sister chromatids toward opposite poles. The kinetochore is the attachment site, but the fibers themselves generate the pulling force. [citation:4]

questions.question: Select the correct statement with respect to mitosis.
options: Chromatids separate but remain in the centre of the cell in anaphase.
Chromatids start moving towards opposite poles in telophase.
Golgi complex and endoplasmic reticulum are still visible at the end of prophase.
Chromosomes move to the spindle equator and get aligned along equatorial plate in metaphase.
questions.explanation: During metaphase, chromosomes align at the equatorial plate (metaphase plate) with spindle fibers attached to kinetochores. Chromatids separate and move to poles in anaphase, not telophase. Golgi and ER fragment and are not visible at the end of prophase. [citation:9]

questions.question: Which of the following best defines a chromatid?
options: a chromosome in the G₁ phase of the cell cycle
one of the two copies of a replicated chromosome
a special region that holds two centromeres together
an alternative term for chromosomes used in genetics
questions.explanation: A chromatid is one of the two identical copies of a replicated chromosome, joined together by a centromere. After DNA replication in S phase, each chromosome consists of two sister chromatids. They separate during anaphase to become individual chromosomes in daughter cells.

questions.question: Starting from a single fertilized egg (zygote), how many cells will be present in the early embryo after five complete cell divisions?
questions.explanation: Each cell division doubles the number of cells. Starting with 1 cell (zygote), after: 1 division → 2 cells, 2 divisions → 4 cells, 3 divisions → 8 cells, 4 divisions → 16 cells, 5 divisions → 32 cells. The formula is 2ⁿ where n = number of divisions. 2⁵ = 32 cells.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is not true?
options: With end of cytokinesis, mitosis is considered to be completed.
In animal cell, the division starts as a furrow in the plasma membrane which deepens to progress and meet in the centre to cut out two free cells.
Cytokinesis procedure is similar in both plant and animal cells.
The two daughter cells are exactly similar to the parent cell in mitosis.
questions.explanation: Cytokinesis differs between plant and animal cells. Animal cells form a cleavage furrow using an actomyosin ring, while plant cells form a cell plate from Golgi-derived vesicles that becomes the new cell wall. [citation:8]

questions.question: In which stage of cell division, the chromosomes are most condensed?
questions.explanation: Chromosomes are most condensed during metaphase, making them easiest to observe and study for karyotyping. While condensation begins in prophase, it reaches maximum compaction by metaphase. [citation:9]

questions.question: Division of centromere occurs in
questions.explanation: The centromere divides during the transition from late metaphase to early anaphase. This allows sister chromatids to separate and move to opposite poles. The division is triggered by separase cleaving cohesin proteins that hold sister chromatids together. [citation:10]

questions.question: Who coined the term mitosis?
questions.explanation: Walther Flemming (1882) coined the term 'mitosis' (from the Greek word 'mitos' meaning thread) to describe the process of nuclear division. He was also the first to describe chromosome behavior during cell division.

scoreMessages.90: Outstanding! You've mastered advanced concepts in cell division! 🔬
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the differences between metaphase and anaphase.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on crossing over stages and cytokinesis differences.
scoreMessages.0: Please review S phase events, chromosome behavior, and mitosis terminology.
